note: RESOLVED 2026-06-12 by web research. Fragment updated: delegated act for EV battery CFD methodology (draft consulted April 2024) and industrial battery delegated act (JRC technical report April 2025) remain unpublished in OJ as of 2026-06-12. The 18 Feb 2025 / 18 Feb 2026 indicative dates in the Regulation are conditional — application starts 12/18 months after OJ publication of BOTH the delegated act and implementing act. Summary and mandatory_status.target updated in besseu-battreg.json to reflect conditionality. Sources: EUR-Lex CELEX:32023R1542; Commission Delegated Act Ares(2024)3131389 (draft, consulted April 2024); EC Environment batteries page (confirms no final OJ publication of CFD delegated acts as of 2026-06-12); EPLCA JRC page.

Source URL valid and official. Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 Articles 18–24 and Annexes VIII–IX on CE marking and conformity assessment are correctly cited. mandatory_status.target is accurate — CE marking is mandatory for all batteries placed on EU market covered by the Regulation. Gap analysis (CCC vs EU conformity assessment) is accurate. No material errors found.

CONFIRMED 2026-06-12 by web research. Fragment already correctly states 18 August 2027 (postponed from 18 August 2025 by Regulation (EU) 2025/1561). Regulation (EU) 2025/1561 adopted 18 July 2025, published OJ 30 July 2025 — confirmed by EUR-Lex CELEX:32025R1561 and multiple sources (infodpp.eu, Consilium press release 2025-07-18). Article 48(1) of Reg 2023/1542 amended to replace 18 August 2025 with 18 August 2027. No further change needed in fragments.

CONFIRMED 2026-06-12 by web research. Dates in fragment are correct: basic labelling from 18 August 2025; extended labelling from 18 August 2026; Battery Passport from 18 February 2027 for industrial and EV batteries ≥2 kWh. 18 February 2027 battery passport date confirmed by multiple sources (batterpass.eu, digiprodpass.com, circularise.com). Delegated acts for Battery Passport data attributes remain pending as of 2026-06-12 — caveat [UNVERIFIED] appropriately retained in fragment. No change needed.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32014L0035 is valid and official (LVD 2014/35/EU on EUR-Lex). EN IEC 62619:2022 exists (IEC webstore publication/64073, Edition 2.0, May 2022) and has been adopted by CENELEC as EN IEC 62619:2022 (approved by CENELEC 2022-06-28, supersedes EN 62619:2017). It is listed as a harmonised standard under LVD via Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2023/2723. EN IEC 63056:2020 also exists. mandatory_status.target correctly states LVD CE marking is mandatory. Gap analysis is accurate.

CONFIRMED 2026-06-12 by web research. Fragment already correctly cites IEC 62933-5-2:2025 Ed 2.0, published 9 December 2025 (IEC webstore publication/68297), superseding IEC 62933-5-2:2020 (Ed 1.0). Full title: Electrical energy storage (EES) systems - Part 5-2: Safety requirements for grid-integrated EES systems - Electrochemical-based systems. CENELEC adoption as FprEN IEC 62933-5-2:2025 in progress. Harmonised standard OJ listing status under review (Malamud ruling) — caveat in fragment is appropriate. No change needed to fragment.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32014L0035 is valid and official (LVD 2014/35/EU). LVD 2014/35/EU exists and applies to equipment in the stated voltage ranges. EN IEC 62619, EN IEC 63056, and IEC 62933-5-2 are correctly identified as key harmonised standards. mandatory_status.target correctly states CE marking under LVD is mandatory. Gap analysis (no bilateral MRA, need for EU-recognised lab tests, EU DoC requirement) is accurate and well-supported.

CONFIRMED 2026-06-12 by web research. Fragment source URL https://webstore.iec.ch/en/publication/68297 correctly points to IEC 62933-5-2:2025 Ed 2.0 (published 9 December 2025). Previous verify note flagged URL /publication/61289 as stale — that was an error in the verify note; the fragment already uses the correct URL 68297. IEC 62933-5-2:2025 Ed 2.0 confirmed as the current edition superseding 2020 Ed 1.0. UL 9540A:2023 edition reference in standards_or_laws is plausible (UL 9540A was revised in 2023) — caveat [UNVERIFIED: exact edition year] appropriately retained. No change needed to fragment.

CONFIRMED 2026-06-12 by web research. Fragment correctly cites Rev.8 (2023) with Amendment 1 (2025) as ST/SG/AC.10/11/Rev.8. Official UNECE pages confirm: Rev.8 published 2023 at https://unece.org/transport/dangerous-goods/rev8-files (document ST/SG/AC.10/11/Rev.8); Amendment 1 (ST/SG/AC.10/11/Rev.8/Amend.1) adopted December 2024 by the Committee, published 2025. Fragment source URL https://unece.org/transport/standards/transport/dangerous-goods/un-manual-tests-and-criteria-rev8-2023 is the correct UNECE Rev.8 page. Previous verify note was based on stale data (the fragment had already been updated from Rev.7 to Rev.8). No change needed.

Source URL https://unece.org/transport/dangerous-goods/adr-2025 resolves to the official UNECE ADR 2025 page (confirmed: https://unece.org/adr-2025-files and related pages exist). ADR 2025, IMDG Code Amendment 41-22, and IATA DGR 65th Edition 2024 citations are accurate. Packing instructions P903/P908 and special provisions cited are consistent with ADR 2025 content. mandatory_status.target correctly states these are legally binding. Gap analysis is accurate and well-reasoned. No material errors found.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32016R0631 is valid and official (Commission Regulation (EU) 2016/631 RfG on EUR-Lex). EN 50549-1:2019 and EN 50549-2:2019 exist as CENELEC standards for generating plants connected to LV/MV distribution networks (confirmed via search). Regulation (EU) 2016/631 RfG is correctly described as binding EU law. VDE-AR-N 4105/4110 and G99 are real national grid codes. mandatory_status.target is accurately described. Gap analysis (GB/T 34120 not equivalent, re-testing required, different communication protocols) is accurate.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1542 is valid and official. LVD 2014/35/EU, EMC Directive 2014/30/EU, and Battery Regulation 2023/1542 are correctly cited as the applicable EU framework for BESS CE marking. EN IEC 62619:2022 and EN 61000-6-2/6-4 are valid harmonised standards. mandatory_status.target is accurate. The [UNVERIFIED] caveat on implementing acts for Reg 2023/1542 assessment modules remains appropriate as of June 2026.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32014L0035 is valid and official. LVD Articles 7/Annex III/IV for DoC and technical file are correctly cited. Battery Regulation Articles 17-18 and 77-78 exist. The Battery Passport date is correctly flagged as subject to delegated acts; the confirmed date (18 February 2027) should be noted but the [UNVERIFIED] caveat on technical specifications remains appropriate. mandatory_status.target is accurate. Gap analysis is sound.

Source URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32019R1020 is valid and official (Market Surveillance Regulation). Regulation 2019/1020 Art. 4-5 (authorised representative), Battery Regulation 2023/1542 Art. 56-61 (producer responsibility), and WEEE Directive 2012/19/EU Art. 16-17 are correctly cited. mandatory_status.target accurately describes the mandatory EU authorised representative and producer registration obligations. The [UNVERIFIED] caveat on Reg 2023/1542 producer scope for stationary BESS vs. industrial batteries remains appropriate given pending implementing acts.
